content:
# Philemon Chapter 1 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), pages 223-224 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/PHM_001_page_001.jpg, PHM_001_page_002.jpg

1. Paul, a prisoner of Christ Jesus, and Timothy our brother, to Philemon our beloved and fellow worker,

2. and to Apphia our sister, and to Archippus our fellow soldier, and to the church in your house:

3. Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

4. I thank my God always, making mention of you in my prayers,

5. hearing of your love and the faith which you have toward the Lord Jesus and toward all the saints,

6. that the fellowship of your faith may become effective in the knowledge of every good thing that is in us for Christ.

7. For I have had much joy and comfort in your love, because the hearts of the saints have been refreshed through you, brother.

8. Therefore, though I have much boldness in Christ to command you what is fitting,

9. for love's sake I rather appeal to you — being such a one as Paul, an old man, and now also a prisoner of Christ Jesus —

10. I appeal to you concerning my child, whom I begot in my bonds, Onesimus,

11. who was formerly useless to you, but now is useful both to you and to me,

12. whom I have sent back to you — him, that is, my very heart —

13. whom I wished to keep with me, that on your behalf he might serve me in the bonds of the gospel.

14. But without your consent I wished to do nothing, so that your good deed would not be as by compulsion, but of your own will.

15. For perhaps he was separated from you for a time for this reason, that you might have him back forever,

16. no longer as a slave, but beyond a slave, a beloved brother — especially to me, but how much more to you, both in the flesh and in the Lord.

17. If then you regard me as a partner, receive him as you would me.

18. But if he has wronged you in anything or owes you anything, charge this to me.

19. I, Paul, write this with my own hand: I will repay it — not to mention to you that you owe me even your own self besides.

20. Yes, brother, let me have benefit from you in the Lord; refresh my heart in Christ.

21. Being confident of your obedience, I write to you, knowing that you will do even beyond what I say.

22. And at the same time also prepare a lodging for me, for I hope that through your prayers I shall be granted to you.

23. Epaphras, my fellow prisoner in Christ Jesus, greets you,

24. as do Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, and Luke, my fellow workers.

25. The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit. Amen.

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **v.1 — "prisoner" (desmios):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΑΥΛΟΣ ΔΕΣΜΙΟΣ ΧΡΙΣΤΟΥ ΙΗΣΟΥ — Paul identifies himself not as "apostle" (as in most epistles) but as "prisoner of Christ Jesus." This is a deliberate rhetorical choice: the appeal to Philemon rests not on apostolic authority but on suffering and love. The term desmios ("one bound, a prisoner") is rendered literally.

2. **v.2 — "Apphia our sister":** Sinaiticus reads ΑΦΦΙΑ ΤΗ ΑΔΕΛΦΗ — "Apphia the sister." She is widely understood to be Philemon's wife. The Sinaiticus reading uses the simple form "sister" (adelphe) rather than "beloved" (agapete), which some later manuscripts read.

3. **v.6 — "fellowship of your faith" (koinonia tes pisteos):** The term koinonia carries the sense of sharing, participation, and partnership. "Fellowship" is retained as the formal equivalent. The phrase "in us" (EN HMIN) follows the Sinaiticus reading; some manuscripts read "in you" (en hymin). The Sinaiticus reading "in us" is preserved, suggesting the good that resides in the community of believers collectively.

4. **v.7 — "hearts" (splanchna):** The Greek splanchna literally denotes the inward parts — bowels, intestines — understood as the seat of deep emotion. Rendered "hearts" throughout (vv. 7, 12, 20) as the closest English equivalent for this visceral metaphor of compassion and affection.

5. **v.9 — "an old man" (presbytes):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΡΕΣΒΥΤΗΣ — presbytes, "an old man." Some scholars have proposed emending to presbeutes ("ambassador"), but the manuscript clearly reads presbytes, and this is preserved. Paul's age becomes part of his emotional appeal.

6. **v.10 — "whom I begot in my bonds" (hon egennesa en tois desmois):** The metaphor of spiritual fatherhood — Paul became Onesimus's father in the faith while in prison. The verb gennao ("beget, give birth to") is rendered "begot" to preserve the paternal imagery.

7. **v.11 — Wordplay on "Onesimus":** The name Onesimos means "useful" or "profitable" in Greek. Paul creates a deliberate wordplay: formerly achrestos ("useless") but now euchrestos ("useful") — both to you and to me. This pun is the rhetorical heart of the letter. The English "useless/useful" preserves the antithesis, though the connection to the name is lost.

8. **v.15 — "separated" (echoristhe):** The passive voice (echoristhe, "was separated") is theologically suggestive — Paul avoids saying "he ran away" and instead employs a divine passive, hinting that God's providence was at work in Onesimus's departure.

9. **v.16 — "no longer as a slave" (ouketi hos doulon):** Paul does not explicitly command manumission, but the logic of the letter presses unmistakably in that direction: Onesimus is to be received "beyond a slave, a beloved brother." The phrase hyperano doulou ("beyond a slave") in Sinaiticus is rendered "beyond a slave" to preserve the comparative force.

10. **v.19 — "I write this with my own hand":** Paul takes the pen from his amanuensis to add a personal guarantee — a legal formula of surety. This brief autograph is both a financial pledge and a deeply personal touch.

11. **v.25 — "Amen":** Sinaiticus concludes with ΑΜΗΝ, which is preserved. The final blessing addresses "your spirit" (tou pneumatos hymon) using the plural possessive, extending the benediction to the entire house church addressed in v.2.

12. **Sinaiticus-specific readings:** This translation follows the Sinaiticus text throughout. Notable variants include "our sister" (v.2) rather than "beloved" found in some traditions, and "in us" (v.6) rather than "in you." The text of Philemon in Sinaiticus is remarkably well-preserved across both folios (pages 223-224), with clear letterforms and minimal damage.

— LOGOS, AI Translator
